text.title: Journey Mapping Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every journey-mapping application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The map MUST include all eight components: persona, stages, actions, touchpoints, thoughts, emotions, pain points, opportunities. Omitting any one is a schema violation.
rule.rationale: Omitting emotions produces an action log, not a journey map. Omitting opportunities produces a complaint document, not a design input. All eight together force the artefact to drive action.

rule.statement: Every map cell MUST cite a research artefact id (interview ID, support ticket ID, analytics event id) OR carry the literal value "no data". Ungrounded prose is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Ungrounded cells are team assumptions masquerading as research findings. Forcing citations turns gaps into research priorities rather than invention prompts.

rule.statement: One persona and one journey per map; explicit start and end points named in the scope field. Combining personas is a schema violation.
rule.rationale: Combining personas hides differences and produces a map that represents neither accurately. Undefined endpoints produce maps with no clear resolution.

rule.statement: Stages MUST be named from the user's perspective (Discovering, Evaluating, Deciding), not the company's pipeline (Lead, Opportunity, Closed). 4-8 stages per map.
rule.rationale: Company pipeline names refocus the map on operations instead of experience. The 4-8 stage range keeps the map readable while preserving granularity.

rule.statement: Emotional values MUST come from research evidence (quotes, sentiment, observed behaviour). Sharp dips MUST be preserved — smoothed arcs are a violation. Agents are explicitly prompted for variance, not average sentiment.

rationale.source: NNG — Emotional Journey Map; LLM behaviour empirical

rule.rationale: LLMs and humans both smooth emotional arcs. Real journeys have sharp dips at friction points — that is where redesign effort is justified. Smoothing hides the actionable signal.

rule.statement: Future-state maps MUST carry an explicit type marker "Future State — Design Hypothesis (not validated with users)". Mixing future-state speculation into a current-state map is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Future-state maps generated from team imagination create false consensus that a designed experience matches user needs. The label keeps the distinction visible on the artefact.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
